figures.caption: Fig. 11. CasLayout is capable of generating plausible scenes based on outof-distribution object lists. Even when given input containing numbers of chairs and tables that exceed the dataset’s maximums, CasLayout produces reasonable and well-arranged results.
Fig. 9. Qualitative comparison of completion results, where the first two rows depict completions without floor plan guidance, and the last two rows show completions guided by floor plan information.

Fig. 25. Diverse scenes generated by CasLayout under architectural element constraints (e.g. , walls, doors, and windows). Explicit modeling of these elements allows the output layouts to reserve sufficient operational space, improving the physical plausibility of the scene.
Fig. 5. A gallery of synthesized scenes demonstrating generation quality and diversity.
Table 12. Comparison with different methods on physical plausibility.

Fig. 8. Qualitative comparisons of scene rearrangement are: the upper two rows correspond to completions without floor plan input, and the lower two rows correspond to completions with floor plan guidance.

Fig. 18. User-provided relation-controlled layout generation. Under varying densities of relational graphs, CasLayout is able to produce diverse results that conform to the given controls and reasonably complete the missing parts (marked by dashed lines) in the conditional graph.
Fig. 21. Pipelines for different applications. (1) Generation, (2) Rearrangement, (3) Completion, (4) Graph-conditioned generation.

Fig. 23. Qualitative diversity analysis: (Top) Nearest training samples (based on object type lists) for unconditionally generated scenes. (Middle) Scenes generated with different random seeds under identical room structures. (Bottom) Rearrangement results obtained with different random seeds given identical room structures and object lists.
Fig. 15. Integration with LLM. When LLM produces coherent and contextually appropriate outputs, CasLayout effectively translates these into spatial arrangements that honor the specified constraints.

Fig. 16. Text-controlled layout generation. The control text is converted into a sparse partial scene graph via an LLM. Conditioned on this translated graph, CasLayout can generate a plausible and complete scene.

Fig. 17. Image-controlled layout generation. The control image is converted into a corresponding scene graph via an VLM. Conditioned on this translated graph, CasLayout can generate a consistent 3D scene.

Table 1. Comparison of our method with existing approaches on floor plan conditional generation. The best result is shown in bold, and the second-best is underlined. N/A indicates that the method does not support the specific application scenario or evaluation metric. CasLayout-G is our general model trained with data augmentation, which achieves state-of-the-art performance across various tasks and experimental settings without the need for retraining. CasLayout is a counterpart trained under the same data setting as other methods for a fair comparison.

Table 2. Comparison of our method with existing approaches on layout generation without floor plan. Both our general model and the model trained without floor plan data achieve superior performance compared to other methods including ATISS [Paschalidou et al. 2021], DiffuScene [Tang et al. 2024], and InstructScene [Lin and Mu 2024], across various metrics.

Fig. 2. The pipeline of CasLayout, which consists of four diffusion processes in a cascaded way: furniture type diffusion, furniture property diffusion, relation latent diffusion, and box layout diffusion. All diffusion networks are built upon a series of self-attention layers. The 2D floor plan (as image) serves as conditioning for each diffusion network via cross-attention layers. The output of each diffusion network is used to construct the input for the subsequent diffusion process.
Fig. 19. Schematic illustration of obtaining feature indices via VQ-VAE.
Fig. 14. Layout variations in response to door or window changes under fixed control conditions. Doors are marked in blue, and windows in orange.

Fig. 13. Qualitative examples of editing: (Top) Modifying an object attribute and adjusting its surrounding local layout accordingly, without altering the region within the blue box. (Middle) Removing certain objects (highlighted by the orange box) and adapting the layout to fit the floor plan. (Bottom) Adding new objects while preserving the existing layout.

Fig. 24. Example of error propagation.When the furniture property diffusion produces error sizes (highlighted in orange), the subsequent stages lack the capability to rectify the earlier mistakes, despite synthesizing relatively plausible layout.

Fig. 6. Comparison with LayouGPT [Feng et al. 2023], ATISS [Paschalidou et al. 2021], DiffuScene [Tang et al. 2024], and GLTScene [Li et al. 2024] with floor plan condition. Our generated results almost completely avoid incorrect overlaps and out-of-bound placements, while making full use of the given space.

Fig. 7. Qualitative comparison with SOTA methods, including ATISS [Paschalidou et al. 2021], DiffuScene [Tang et al. 2024], and InstructScene [Lin and Mu 2024]. Our method demonstrates better local plausibility and clearer functional zoning.
